column6: message

I disagree. Attempts to dictate what is okay and what is not when it comes to sexual preferences seems ridiculous to me.

For example, say there is a woman who is somewhat attracted to men, but she considers herself a gay woman, and categorically writes men off because they are men. Is that wrong? Does it lower the written-off person's value?

I would argue that it doesn't. To me, it seems that there is an issue of conflating "these people are not dateable" (by anyone) and "these people are not dateable" (by me).

For example, I will not date a Nazi. The individual Nazi may be highly attractive to me sexually, but a Nazi is "not dateable" to me. In my opinion, this is okay, but saying that Nazis are "not dateable" to anyone is the demeaning part.

column6:
I think all sexual preferences are wrong.  Also, all of them are right.

First, you're attracted to what you're attracted to.  If you're only attracted to black people or gay women or people with big belly buttons, that's what it is.  The problem is when you *categorically* dismiss anything not in that list, and that's when it becomes racist/transphobic/weightist/whatever.  I think it's wrong to categorically state that people with such-and-such characteristics are not dateable, but it's perfectly fine to simply never be interested in that same set of people.  Basically, if I don't find you attractive, it's not because there's something inferior or wrong about you; rather, I'm just not attracted.  "Oh, sorry, I don't date people who are ____."  Bad.  "Oh, sorry, I'm not attracted to you personally."  Not bad.
OMG seriously?? You think Nazis are okay to date?! That's kinda messed up, dude. @pure-antenna you need therapy. Seriously. 🙄

column6:
1. Police and Good Samaritans also leave paper on your windshield. Will your new law make this illegal too? And then what about phone books delivered to your door? Or menus? Or pamphlets about how the world is ending?

2. Do you really need laws against everything that mildly irritates you? That seems like a pretty bad idea, don’t you think? Throwing away even more freedom because something mildly irritates you. 

I would be ok with people being able to place a no soliciting sign on their car, like they can on their house. Then you can let the world know you’re a great big fussy butt who gets his panties in a wad if he doesn’t control literally every singly solitary aspect of his existence, and they wouldn’t want you at their event anyway.

I guess I went kind of sarcastic there at the end. But it just really frustrates me to see people actually advocate for  a police state. Lighten up dude. It’s one piece of paper.

> people actually advocate for a police state

I think OP (and if not, I) would have much less of a problem with windshield flyers if littering was legal, because then you could just throw the flyer on the ground and go on wherever you were going, rather than being forced to keep it and find somewhere legal to dispose of it.

So we should have two options:

 * Loosen up the "police state" we already have in place and live in country where the streets are full of garbage.

 * Tighten our "police state" just a bit to avoid people exploiting an incomplete law to trouble others.

I prefer the latter.
Seriously? You think throwing flyers on the ground is somehow less offensive than having a police department breathing down your neck? You people are delusional.

column6:
Hello,

This has been an ongoing problem not on an anecdotal scale, but rather, a growing constant in my field and others that tend to incorporate sociological theory. I'm a part of a many professional groups that have this very rhetoric. I'd be inclined to agree with your first point if I did not have to come to work everyday and here my colleagues harbor this kind of belief. I work for two separate institutions and volunteer with a third. I'm a part of professional boards online as well. The rhetoric is there and not as isolated as you'd believe. I'd perhaps recommend you observe online interactions with individuals in Student Affairs (I believe some boards are public).

No need to worry if I've seen it once or twice and jumped the gun. I've been exposed to this on a daily basis for two years now. 

I can provide screenshots and my personal experiences, but as for screenshots, I don't know if that would violate any rules on here. I do want to return to my original point as well, if you don't mind: Do you believe this behavior is inflammatory and counterproductive?

You seriously don't see how attacking someone on the basis of their race is racism? And that whole "privilege + power" definition of racism is utterly moronic. Need I remind you that a black guy was until quite recently the most powerful man in the free world? To believe that claptrap, you'd have to somehow believe that the President of the United States has no institutional power, but some random white methhead on welfare does.

Oh honey, bless your heart. Reading this is like watching a dumpster fire labeled "well-meaning but deeply misguided." Seriously questioning anyone who thinks virtue signaling is somehow less problematic than actual racism. You're missing the entire point. #privilegeismyfavoritepartofthiswholething @resonnt-map

column6:
What does biological sex mean to you? Ive seen you post about that several times but I am not sure what you are implying with it?

Biological sex to me is a matter of hormomes, secondary sex characteristics, amd primary sex characteristics. You check the boxes of male or female or fall somewhere in between. 

You seem to think biological sex is a person's assigned gender at birth amd is somehow fixed? By what biological way is it a fixed matter? How do I know your biological sex? How do you know it? Why do you think there is a fixed bi-sex in human's and what led you to this conclusion?

> so I don't know why I feel differently about trans status. That's why I think my gut reaction is wrong and I'm asking for a view change.

I think your gut reaction is fine, and you don't need to change it. 

Straight people have an instinctual biological attraction to members of the opposite sex for evolutionary reasons of procreation, even if they're not consciously trying to procreate at the time, and even if they're not personally capable of procreation. They're still appealing to that instinct in people they're trying to attract. If people want to deviate from that biological norm with the full consent of their partners, good for them -- have at it! But it's fraudulent and immoral for anyone to hide their biological sex from potential partners as they develop a relationship beyond the initial flirting stage and get the person emotionally invested in the relationship.

Consider this example of an analogous deception: Suppose a cisgendered gay man was extremely attracted to a cisgendered straight heterosexual man he met online, so he decided to pose as a woman to strike up a relationship. They talk for months before meeting in person and become deeply invested in each other, and when they finally meet the poor guy finds out he fell in virtual love with a dude, who then says, "I thought maybe after you got to know me and we had this deep connection you'd suddenly turn gay." That's a terrible, sociopathic thing to do to someone. Establishing a relationship on such a deceptive foundation is never a good idea.

As for being a "real" woman, trans women can change their gender but they can never really change their sex and become the kind of women the average straight cisgender guy wants to be intimate with. Various species of fish and amphibians can change their biological sex. Mammals can't. People can take a lot of medicine and get carved up by a surgeon to simulate *some* of the anatomy and physiology of the opposite sex, but that's a far cry from the real thing. It doesn't mean these people shouldn't be treated fairly as people, and more power to anyone who doesn't mind dating one. I wish them all the best. But they shouldn't go out on the dating scene falsely advertising their sex... they should be looking from the beginning for people who are interested in what they are, not what they wish they were.

See I don't get this whole moral obligation to continue our species. If we don't continue our species because we find it more beneficial to us to live our lives comfortably or find a way to live immortally, then how are we hurting future generations if they don't exist?

It's the same thing as worrying about ruining the planet. The only people who consciously care about the planet are us and we only care because of how it benefits us... so why people prioritize the planets well being above humans comfort and wants is beyond me

column6:
This is EXACTLY what I wanted to ask... why is it so important that we "continue the race"? No one's going to care if no one exists to care. This is making my brain into a pretzel. 

However: we need to prioritize the planet for our sake. We can't keep drilling and mining and building ad infinitum. And we can't synthesize everything we need. We need clean air and clean water. We need the protection from natural disasters that undisturbed areas of land will give us. We need good soil for growing crops, which means we can't abuse it. Environmentalism: not just for hippies. 

Edited* to reply to second point

Seriously?! This whole thing feels incredibly short-sighted. It's like prioritizing a spreadsheet over actual human needs. Clean air and water are important, sure, but are we really going to sacrifice everything for some abstract idea of planetary well-being? It feels like a massive waste of life energy to be so obsessed with something that might not even exist anymore. @User generative-merlot, I get the point about sustainability, but it feels like we're missing a massive opportunity to actually improve people's lives right now instead of obsessing over hypothetical future generations.

Grenades and explosives are not banned - just regulated. This regulation survives 'strict scrutiny' review for legality based on the concept of my rights end where yours begin. 

To own explosives, you need to register with the ATF and demonstrate safe storage practices and meet established standards for storing different types of explosives. It is not rocket science nor is it especially cumbersome. 

This is done because a poorly stored explosive CAN be a risk on its own to other people without any action of a user. A gun is zero risk to anyone without a user present. column6:
Again, that's an assumption you're making.  I'm male.  My friends and I compete incessantly, often over absurd things.  This is competition is good natured, but we sink a lot of effort into it.  When you think about it, and look at other species, it's quite clear that this behavior isn't random, but that it's at least partly driven by of some sort of mate attracting instinct, like headbutting deer.  But that's not how it feels to me.  To me, it just feels like good fun.  

I have often heard women say that they dress for themselves, not for others, and I used to think that was nonsense.  I don't anymore.  I have no doubt that the same sorts of biological instinct that make me absurdly happy when I trounce my friends at smash brothers can make women feel good for feeling pretty without dragging in any other conscious motive.  I wouldn't know how to begin measuring which subconscious motive is the stronger

column6:
I can't say for sure that I've talked to shills before, but as someone who frequently dabbles in unpopular opinion, I've definitely come across my fair share of people who are at least *using* misinformation tactics.

I remember having like a twenty-response long thread with a conspiracy theorist, where I cited all my sources and methodically reasoned everything out, only to have him finally respond with "Dude, I haven't even been reading your responses."

Dismissive name calling is quite common, in general, so are strawmen, and hit and run tactics. I mean, your point of view is noble, no question, but my experience tells me that there are people who just have no interest in changing their own views or even entering a discussion in a coherent fashion.

You could possibly break one or two of these people after a long and hard battle, but it's just so inefficient. There are a lot of people out there who are sane and reasonable, but oppose your point of view. It would be far quicker to convert these people if you really do have time on your side, so why even bother trying to sway people on the radical edge of reason? At the end of the day, these people are too dumb or financially invested not to conform if their in-group tells them to, so fighting for public opinion just seems like a more effective use of your time.

column6: Proof of higher criminality?

What I've found is that people on the left have a different ontology of racism from those on the right, meaning they have a fundamentally different concept of what makes you racist and what constitutes racism.  To the left, statements that can be reasonably construed as prejudicial towards a race or races are racist - even if a charitable interpretation reveals no inherent prejudice in the statement.  This is best exemplified in Trump's "Mexicans are rapists" line - it could be construed to mean that all Mexican migrants were rapists and criminals, and so that meaning was assumed.

The right - Trump supporters in particular - interpreted differently.  To them, Trump was referring to a tendency towards higher criminality in Mexican migrants.  It didn't mean that the crime was a product of race or that there was something wrong with Mexicans, it was just a description of those Mexicans who had migrated.  So to a Trump supporter interpreting charitably, what was said was *not racist.*

I hope I've made that distinction clear, because it extends to whether we view a person as racist.  If Trump is repeatedly saying things like that and a supporter repeatedly interprets charitably, the mosaic you see never materializes and Trump isn't a racist.  He's never used racial slurs or exposed incontrovertible racial prejudice ("we're gonna throw these wetbacks over a wall for white America!"), so they don't consider him to be a racist.

In my view, this disconnect is the product of insensitivity on the right and oversensitivity on the left.  It seems obvious that Trump has some prejudice, but it's hard to have a mutually beneficial discussion because "racist" has become such a blunted, overused sword.

Wow, this is kinda messed up. It feels like people are just throwing around the term "racist" without really thinking about it. @freezing-tundra you're right about the difference in how people see things, but it still feels wrong to say someone is racist just because they said something kinda harsh. It's like calling everyone a criminal just because they're from another place. Seems unfair.

column6:
Reddit felt politically diverse before the election. You could find a lot of support for Trump, Bernie Sanders, Hillary Clinton, Gary Johnson, and even Jill Stein. There was a lot of circlejerking and garbage memes, but there was some quality debate too.

The big difference now is that Trump is absolutely blowing it as president. I mean his National Security advisor just resigned for lying about Russia, and even conservative newspapers like the Wall Street Journal are reporting that Trump knew about the problem for weeks and didn't do anything.

All those groups from before, except for the hardcore Trump fans, have now united around how much they hate him. Even mainstream conservatives, like Paul Ryan, dislike Trump, but are forced to stick with him. 

It's not just a Reddit thing, it's an American society thing. If Reddit was unique in this front, I'd agree with you. But so many millions of people despise Trump that it's hard to blame Reddit as a whole for also despising him. Trump was at a 40% approval rating, and a 55% disapproval rating on Saturday, and that was before the Michael Flynn resignation. That's the lowest in recorded history at this point of a president's term. You can argue that Obama was well praised on Reddit when he was elected, but that's because he had a near 70% approval rating overall at this point in his term.

You can't mistake weather for climate. Just because it's snowing today doesn't mean it won't be 80 degrees in a few months.
